Aggregation sensors have the same parameters as the metering sensor object, but the `input_name` must be `composite`.

For this example, we will use a vehicle with two fuel level sensors installed in two different tanks. The vehicle has a maximum fuel capacity of 340 liters.

Create a sensor for fuel tank 1 and another for fuel tank 2. Take their `sensor_ids` to use in the `parent_ids` parameter.

For the fuel composite sensor, we don't use parameters responsible for fuel level change speed - `max_lowering_by_time` and `max_lowering_by_mileage`. The platform will take the default `max_lowering_by_time` = 120 L/h to determine drains in reports.

We should set the full tank value into the `volume` parameter, and `parent_ids` must be from fuel level sensors in tank 1 and tank 2.

Metering sensors are sensors that may provide valuable readings, such as temperature, mileage, fuel level, and so on. The metering sensor has the following object structure:

``` json
{
    "type": "metering",
    "id": 860250,
    "sensor_type": "temperature",
    "name": "OBD Coolant temperature",
    "input_name": "obd_coolant_t",
    "multiplier": 1.0,
    "divider": 1.0,
    "accuracy": 0.0,
    "units": "",
    "units_type": "celsius",
    "parameters": {
      "parent_ids": [123042, 123566],
      "volume": 0.7,
      "min": 0.0,
      "max": 12.0,
      "max_lowering_by_time": 120.0,
      "max_lowering_by_mileage": 120.0,
      "ignore_drains_in_move": true,
      "ignore_refuels_in_move": false,
      "refuel_gap_minutes": 11
    }
}

 ```

Field definitions:

- `type` - string. Always `metering` for metering sensors.
    
- `id` - integer. A sensor id.
    
- `sensor_type` - string. Type of a metering sensor. Listed below.
    
- `name` - string. A name of a sensor.
    
- `input_name` - string. The input name. Don't mix it up with the `sensor_type`. The `input_name` is where the platform receives the readings. You can see `input_names` on the right part of the Air console. Also, you can get the list of all supported `input_types` by the platform [here](https://developers.navixy.com/backend-api/resources/tracking/tracker/sensor/input_name/#response).  
    Should be `composite` for aggregation sensors. Will be described additionally.
    
- `divider` - double. Divider is used to divide the incoming values from the sensor. For example, if your sensor sends 120, and with a divider of 10, the platform will save 12 into the sensor's data.
    
- `accuracy` - integer. The sensor's accuracy in percentages. It is used for the fuel sensor type only. For others, you may set 0. It is necessary to count the possible absolute error to define refills or drains of fuel. We have described it in detail in the [Eco Fleet docs section](https://docs.navixy.com/eco-fleet/sensors-setup-and-configuration). The minimum=0.0, maximum=100.0 with a step of 0.25.
    
- `units` - string. What units should be shown on the platform. Should be used with `units_type` `custom` only. For other types, it should be empty.
    
- `units_type` - string. Units type. May be `custom` for all `sensor_type`. Other available unit types are listed below.
    
- `parameters` - object. Optional object with additional parameters.
    
    - `parent_ids` - optional array of parent_ids for `composite` `input_type`.
        
    - `volume` - double. Max volume of a composite sensor, applicable for `composite` `input_type` only.
        
    - `min` - double. The platform will ignore raw data from this sensor less than this value.
        
    - `max` - double. The platform will ignore raw data from this sensor more than this value.
        
    - `max_lowering_by_time` - integer. The maximum allowable flow rate is measured in units per hour. When calculating the fuel change speed over time, the platform compares the fuel level change between points. If it is not set, the default value is 120 units per hour. It means the fuel level should change faster than 120 per hour (equal to 20 L per 10 minutes or 2 L per minute) to determine the drain in a report. This value should be set a few percentage points higher than the likely consumption rate during heavy loads or when the vehicle is ascending uphill. Parameter is applicable for `sensor_type` `fuel` only.
        
    - `max_lowering_by_mileage` - integer. The maximum allowable fuel level change speed is measured in units per 100 km. For example, if we set 100 L per 100 km, it means the fuel level should change faster than 100 L per 100 km (equal to 10 L per 10 km or 1 L per km) to determine the drain in a report. We recommend conducting tests and verifying the actual fuel consumption rate recorded in the reports, then set the necessary values accordingly for maximum accuracy. Parameter is applicable for `sensor_type` `fuel` only.
        
    - `ignore_drains_in_move` - boolean. Drains in movement will be excluded. Parameter is applicable for `sensor_type` `fuel` only.
        
    - `ignore_refuels_in_move` - boolean. Refills in movement will be filtered. Parameter is applicable for `sensor_type` `fuel` only.
        
    - `refuel_gap_minutes` - integer. This setting is required when the Ignore feature is enabled. It determines the timeout period in minutes that will be used to shorten the driving intervals for fuel filtering. This option can be helpful if the fuel level stabilizes only after some time has passed since refueling, and the vehicle has already started moving. This is more commonly seen in vehicles with large fuel tanks. The default setting for this feature is 5 minutes. Parameter is applicable for `sensor_type` `fuel` only.
        

Metering sensor types with available for them `units_type`:

- `fuel`
    
    - `litres`
        
    - `gallon_imp` - gallon (UK).
        
    - `gallon_us` - gallon (US).
        
    - `percent`
        
    - `custom`
        
- `temperature`
    
    - `celsius`

- `custom`

- `fuel_consumption`
    
    - `lp100km` - liters per 100 km.
        
    - `mpg_imp` - miles per gallon (UK).
        
    - `mpg_us` - miles per gallon (US).
        
    - `kml` - kilometers per liter.
        
    - `gp100mile_imp` - gallons (UK) per 100 miles.
        
    - `gp100mile_us` - gallons (US) per 100 miles.
        
    - `kmpg_us` - kilometers per gallons (US).
        
    - `gp100km_imp` - gallons (UK) per 100 km.
        
    - `gp100km_us` - gallons (US) per 100 km.
        
    - `lph` - liters per hour.
        
    - `litre_per_nmi` - liters per nautical mile.
        
    - `gph_imp` - gallons (UK) per hour.
        
    - `gph_us` - gallons (US) per hour.
        
    - `custom`
        
- `instant_consumption`
    
    - `lp100km` - liters per 100 km.
        
    - `mpg_imp` - miles per gallon (UK).
        
    - `mpg_us` - miles per gallon (US).
        
    - `kml` - kilometers per liter.
        
    - `gp100mile_imp` - gallons (UK) per 100 miles.
        
    - `gp100mile_us` - gallons (US) per 100 miles.
        
    - `kmpg_us` - kilometers per gallons (US).
        
    - `gp100km_imp` - gallons (UK) per 100 km.
        
    - `gp100km_us` - gallons (US) per 100 km.
        
    - `lph` - liters per hour.
        
    - `litre_per_nmi` - liters per nautical mile.

- `power`

- `acceleration`

Further we will describe most common sensor types with examples.

item.name: Discrete inputs
item.description:
Discrete inputs are device inputs where you can connect ignition state, SOS button, door sensor, or something similar that may have only two states - 1 or 0 - enabled/disabled, active/inactive.

Discrete input object:

``` json
{
  "type": "discrete",
  "id": 888951,
  "sensor_type": "ignition",
  "name": "Ignition",
  "input_number": 4
}

 ```

- `type` - string. Always `discrete`.
    
- `id` - integer. An ID of a sensor. In other requests should be used as `sensor_id`.
    
- `sensor_type` - enum string. Discrete sensor type.
    - `ignition`
        
    - `sos_button`
        
    - `power`
        
    - `engine`
        
    - `car_alarm`
        
    - `door`
        
    - `charge`
        
    - `detach`
        
    - `custom`
        
- `name`\- string. Sensor name. Max size 100 symbols.
    
- `input_number` - int. Available from 1 to 8. To which input number of your device this sensor is assigned.

item.name: Virtual sensors
item.description:
Virtual sensors may be used to represent standard sensor readings with your definitions. For example, you can use "cold" or "hot" for temperature sensors, "need refueling" and "enough fuel" for fuel level sensors, and so on.

There are three calculation methods for virtual sensors:

- Value in range where can set a specific range for your definitions. For example, more than 20 L is "enough fuel," and less than 20 L is "need refueling."
    
- Specific value where you can set definitions to any specific values from your sensor. For example, you receive only several values from the sensor and define them as "danger," "normal," or "perfect."
    
- Bit index where you can get the value of a specific bit from a field that comes in the packet. For example, the device provides different states (1/0) in one field: ignition, movement, door state, hood state, and so on. The platform receives 1010101 in one field, so getting a specific state is not possible using measurement sensors. We need only one bit responsible for the movement state. We should set this bit index virtual sensor and can get information about the movement state only.
    

Additional plus - one virtual sensor per tracker may be used as an ignition state. The platform will then calculate all engine hours and parking detection with considering ignition based on this sensor state. For example, you can set up ignition by the board voltage, movement, and anything else you think is more suitable for getting the ignition state in your business case instead of discrete input 1.

### **Virtual sensor object**

``` json
{
  "type": "virtual",
  "id": 1700049,
  "sensor_type": "virtual_ignition",
  "name": "Virtual Ignition",
  "input_name": "board_voltage",
  "parameters": {
    "calc_method": "in_range",
    "range_from": 13.4,
    "value_titles": [{
        "value": "0",
        "title": "Off"
    }, {
        "value": "1",
        "title": "On"
    }]
  }
}

 ```

Where:

- `type` - string. Always "virtual" for virtual sensors.
    
- `id` - integer. Sensor's id.
    
- `sensor_type` - string. Virtual sensor type. Type of the sensor. "virtual_ignition" for virtual ignition or "state" for others.
    
- `name` - string. A name of sensor. Max size 100 symbols.
    
- `input_name` - string. A source input field name (identifier). Max size 64 symbols.
    
- `parameters` - an object with additional parameters.
    
    - `calc_method` - string. A method of sensor value calculation. One of this: "in_range", "identity", "bit_index".
        
    - `range_from` - double. Low bound of range. It is used only with "in_range" calc method.
        
    - `range_to` - double. High bound of range. It is used only with "in_range" calc method.
        
    - `bit_index` - integer. A bit index in input field source value. It is used only with "bit_index" calc method.
        
    - `value_titles` - mapping for bind special titles for sensor values, if it is necessary.
        
        - `value` - string. Sensor value - what the sensor provides to the platform. Max size 64 symbols.
            
        - `title` - string. Title for the sensor value - what will you see in widgets. Max size 64 symbols.
            

### Some requirements

- There can only be one virtual sensor with the type `virtual_ignition` for a tracker.
    
- One or both fields `range_from` and `range_to` must be present for the calculation method `in_range`.
    
- Field `bit_index` must be present for the calc method `bit_index`.
    
- There can be no more than 100 value titles.
    
- All values must be unique within `value_titles`. There can't be one value with two definitions.

We described work with virtual sensors and different cases of use in our [instructions](https://developers.navixy.com/backend-api/how-to/virtual-sensors/).

In our example, we will add and update a virtual ignition by board voltage.
Here you will learn how to create and update different types of sensors.

item.name: Reading sensors data
Last readings from all sensors on one tracker
Last readings from all sensors on several trackers

item.description:
Once you have created sensors on the platform and your device has provided this data to the platform, you can request readings from sensors.

There are several ways to get sensor data:

1\. If you need data from a particular type of sensors:

- [get_diagnostics](https://developers.navixy.com/backend-api/resources/tracking/tracker/#get_diagnostics) - to get last readings from CAN and OBD sensors .
    
- [get_fuel](https://developers.navixy.com/backend-api/resources/tracking/tracker/#get_fuel) - to get last updated data from fuel sensors.
    
- [get_inputs ](https://developers.navixy.com/backend-api/resources/tracking/tracker/#get_inputs) \- to request input states.
    

2\. If you need data from all tracker sensors

- [readings/list](https://developers.navixy.com/backend-api/resources/tracking/tracker/readings/#list) - to request the last readings from all sensors of one tracker
    
- [readings/batch_list](https://developers.navixy.com/backend-api/resources/tracking/tracker/readings/#batch_list) - to request the last readings from all sensors of different sensors.
    

3\. In case you need historical data from specific sensor for a specified period use [sensor/data/read](https://developers.navixy.com/backend-api/resources/tracking/tracker/sensor/#dataread) API request.

We will share all these requests further.

The ability to connect sensors, as well as their number, may vary depending on the device model. Some sensors are automatically created by the platform. The list of these sensors depends on the device model and the information received from them. Some sensors, such as the board voltage, will be added automatically during the registration process, but most of other sensors should be created manually.

Full sensor creation has several steps:

1. Data sending from the sensor should be configured on the device's side, and it should be received by the platform. To check which sensor is received by the platform, we recommend using [the air console in the admin panel](https://docs.navixy.com/admin-panel/air-console) in the admin panel as the fastest way with a GUI.  
    It is also possible to connect via [websockets to console](https://developers.navixy.com/panel-api/resources/tracker/#consoleconnect), but it requires additional websocket configuration on your side.
    
2. After checking the air console, we know which sensor sends data to the platform (the right console part) and can choose one of them to [create](https://developers.navixy.com/backend-api/resources/tracking/tracker/sensor/#create).
    
3. The sensor should be additionally [calibrated](https://developers.navixy.com/backend-api/resources/tracking/tracker/sensor/calibration_data/) in case it sends data in an uncalibrated form. For example, analog temperature or LLS fuel level sensors.
    

There are several different sensor sub-types:

- Metering sensors - are sensors that may provide valuable readings, such as temperature, mileage, and fuel level. They can also be aggregation (composite) sensors that sum or average readings from sensors of the same type.
    
- Discrete inputs - are digital inputs which can get on/off states from wires.
    
- Virtual sensor - the additional sensor which can get information from real sensors, but you're able to define your values for the standard readings. For example, if it gets data from a fuel level sensor, you can set the values - "need to refill" or "enough fuel".
    

We will describe them all further and provide examples for creating and updating them.
Here, you will find various API calls to work with trackers that are registered under a user account.

All API calls documented in this collection are associated with the user account and all the entities within it. In other words, everything you can find in the web user interface is described below.

Each API resource is semantically linked to a specific entity, such as geofences, rules, objects, and so on. The API calls for CRUD (Create, Read, Update, and Delete) operations, as well as other operations with these entities, have similar names regardless of the resource being used. These names include "list", "read", "create", "update", and "delete".
